title: Tamil Vatha Kuzhambu

ingredients.name: tamarind
visual_checks: soak in 250 ml warm water and extract pulp — this gravy needs generous sourness
ingredients.notes: soak in 250 ml warm water and extract pulp — this gravy needs generous sourness

ingredients.name: gingelly oil (nallennai)
visual_checks: do not substitute — gingelly oil defines the flavour
ingredients.notes: do not substitute — gingelly oil defines the flavour

subs.name: coconut oil as second choice — the flavour will differ significantly
equal quantity of homemade blend: 2 parts coriander + 1 part cumin + 1 part red chilli
2 drumstick pieces or 100 g raw banana, cubed
subs.notes: drumstick adds a nice bitterness that echoes vathal

steps.text: Soak the tamarind in 250 ml warm water for 15 minutes. Squeeze out all the pulp thoroughly, strain, and set the extract aside. This dish depends on a strong tamarind base, so don't rush this step.

Heat gingelly oil in a heavy-bottomed kadai over medium flame. Splutter mustard seeds, then add fenugreek seeds and fry for 20 seconds — they should turn golden, not dark brown, or they will taste bitter. Add dried red chillies, curry leaves, and asafoetida.
Add the shallots and cook on medium flame until they soften and develop a light golden colour, about 5 minutes. If using brinjal, add it now and fry for 3 minutes until the edges begin to colour.
Lower the flame and add the red chilli powder, coriander powder, sambar powder, and turmeric. Stir continuously for 30 seconds so the powders toast in the oil without burning.

Pour in the tamarind extract and add salt. Bring to a boil on high flame, then reduce to a lively simmer. Cook uncovered for 15–18 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the gravy reduces to a thick, glossy consistency and the oil rises to the surface.

Taste for salt and sourness. The finished kuzhambu should be quite thick — almost like a sauce — dark reddish-brown, and intensely flavoured. Serve with rice and a plain dal; a spoonful goes a long way.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.

substitution_notes: brinjal → 2 drumstick pieces or 100 g raw banana, cubed (drumstick adds a nice bitterness that echoes vathal); gingelly-oil → coconut oil as second choice — the flavour will differ significantly; sambar-powder → equal quantity of homemade blend: 2 parts coriander + 1 part cumin + 1 part red chilli
